flashcards.front: What is the primary problem with binary search trees that balanced search trees solve?
flashcards.back: BSTs degrade to O(n) time complexity in worst case when elements are inserted in sorted order, creating a skewed tree. Balanced trees maintain O(log n) worst-case complexity.

flashcards.front: What is the balance factor in AVL trees?
flashcards.back: Balance factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). For AVL trees, it must be -1, 0, or +1 for all nodes.

flashcards.front: What are the four rotation cases in AVL tree insertion?
flashcards.back: LL (Left-Left): right rotation; RR (Right-Right): left rotation; LR (Left-Right): left rotation then right rotation; RL (Right-Left): right rotation then left rotation.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum height of an AVL tree with n nodes?
flashcards.back: The maximum height is approximately 1.44 * log2(n+2) - 0.328, which is O(log n). This ensures O(log n) operations.

flashcards.front: What are the five properties of Red-Black trees?
flashcards.back: 1) Every node is red or black; 2) Root is black; 3) Every leaf (NIL) is black; 4) Red node cannot have red children; 5) All paths from node to descendant leaves have same black height.

flashcards.front: What is the height of a Red-Black tree with n nodes?
flashcards.back: The height is at most 2*log2(n+1), which is O(log n). This guarantees O(log n) time for search, insert, and delete operations.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of AVL tree operations?
flashcards.back: Search, insertion, and deletion all take O(log n) time in worst, average, and best cases.
A balanced search tree where each node can have 2 or 3 children. All leaves are at the same level. It maintains perfect balance with O(log n) operations.

flashcards.front: What is a B-tree of order m?
flashcards.back: A balanced multi-way search tree where each node can have at most m children and between m/2 and m-1 keys (except root). All leaves are at same level.

flashcards.front: Why are B-trees suitable for disk storage?
flashcards.b ack: B-trees have high branching factor, reducing tree height. Since disk I/O is expensive, minimizing node accesses (logm n) makes B-trees efficient for large databases.

flashcards.front: What is a tree rotation in AVL trees?
flashcards.back: A rotation is a local restructuring operation that changes tree structure while maintaining BST property. It involves re-arranging nodes around an unbalanced node to restore balance.

flashcards.front: What is double rotation in AVL trees?
flashcards.back: Double rotation is needed for LR and RL cases. It consists of two single rotations: first rotate the child in opposite direction, then rotate the unbalanced node.

flashcards.front: What happens during Red-Black tree insertion when the parent is red?
flashcards.back: If parent is red, the tree has a red-red violation. Cases: if uncle is red, recolor; if uncle is black and triangle, rotate; if uncle is black and line, rotate and recolor.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 2-3 trees and B-trees?
flashcards.back: 2-3 trees are a special case of B-trees with order 3 (max 3 children). B-trees generalize this with order m, suitable for disk-based storage with larger m.

flashcards.front: Why prefer AVL trees over Red-Black trees for lookup-intensive applications?
flashcards.back: AVL trees are more strictly balanced (height difference ≤1) than Red-Black trees, providing faster lookups (approximately 44% more comparisons in worst case) at the cost of more rotations during insertions.
